steps.title: Separate Hans from the story
steps.content: The narrator says the story is told not for Hans Castorp's sake, since the reader will come to know him as ordinary, but for the sake of the story itself. That makes the story's value the main point rather than Hans's personal importance.

steps.title: Read the parenthetical caveat
steps.content: The parenthetical complicates that first idea: it notes that the story is still Hans Castorp's story and that not every story happens to everybody. In other words, the story is valuable on its own, but its value gives Hans some significance because he is the person at its center.

steps.title: Reject unsupported claims
steps.content: Choice A says the story's interest is difficult to understand because of time, which the passage does not say. Choice B treats age and tense as the signs of importance but misses the credit given to Hans. Choice D makes a broad claim about all inconsequential people that the passage never supports.

steps.title: Match the caveat
steps.content: The remaining choice captures both parts of the narrator's logic: the story is remarkable even though Hans is ordinary, and some of the story's value can be connected back to him because it is his story. The answer is C.
